title: Msemen
subtitle: Moroccan flaky square flatbread
excerpt: Thin dough folded many times with butter and semolina to create a flaky, layered flatbread, then cooked on a dry griddle until spotted with brown on each side. The layers pull apart as you tear it. Served at breakfast with honey and butter, or alongside tagines instead of khobz.
sourceNotes: Msemen (also called meloui in a round version) is a laminated flatbread found across the Maghreb. The folding technique creates distinct layers separated by semolina and butter or oil. It is served as part of the Moroccan breakfast spread alongside honey, argan oil, and amlou (almond and argan paste). The square shape distinguishes msemen from the round rghaif.

content.text: The technique is a fold-and-stretch process, not a kneaded dough. Each ball of dough is stretched flat, spread with softened butter and a dusting of semolina, then folded in thirds both ways to create a layered square. The semolina keeps the layers separate as the msemen cooks.

Use oil on your hands and surface rather than flour when stretching, flour would absorb into the layers and make them dense. The dough should be stretched as thin as you can without tearing. The final square should be about 15 cm across before going into the pan.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{semolina}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add the {{water}} gradually, mixing to a soft, smooth dough. Knead for 5 minutes until elastic. Divide into 8 balls. Coat with {{vegetable-oil}}, cover, and rest for 30 minutes.

Oil your work surface and hands. Take one dough ball and stretch it flat with oiled fingers into the thinnest possible round without tearing. Spread a teaspoon of {{unsalted-butter}} over the surface and dust lightly with {{semolina}}. Fold the right third over the centre, then the left third over that (like a letter). Spread another small amount of butter on top and dust with semolina. Fold the bottom third up, then the top third down to make a small square. Set aside and repeat with the remaining dough balls. Heat a dry griddle or heavy frying pan over medium heat. Cook each msemen for 3 to 4 minutes per side, pressing lightly with a spatula, until golden and spotted with brown. Serve immediately with honey and butter.

items.fix: The butter layer must cover the whole stretched surface. Make the folds sharp and press firmly. The semolina between layers is what keeps them separate
items.cause: Not enough butter between folds, or folds not sharp

items.fix: Rest the dough balls for the full 30 minutes. Keep hands and surface well-oiled. Never use flour. It dries the dough and causes tearing
items.cause: Not rested long enough, or oil insufficient

items.fix: Medium heat, not high. Press with a spatula during cooking to ensure even contact with the pan. Each side needs at least 3 minutes
items.cause: Heat too high or too low, not pressed during cooking

content.text: Msemen is the bread of Moroccan breakfast, served with mint tea, honey, butter, and amlou (a paste of ground almonds, argan oil, and honey). In Moroccan cities, msemen sellers set up their griddles early in the morning and cook to order. In homes it is a weekend or holiday breakfast, made fresh and eaten immediately. It is also sold stuffed with meat or cheese as a street snack. Outside Morocco it is increasingly made in North African diaspora homes in France and Britain, and it requires no special equipment beyond a flat griddle or pan.
